content.text: Tool learning studies how models acquire, select, and compose external tools — APIs, code interpreters, search engines, databases — to extend their capabilities beyond what their weights encode. It reframes the model as a controller that orchestrates software rather than a closed oracle expected to know everything.
Tool learning is the research program concerned with teaching models to use tools effectively: deciding
to interpret and chain its outputs. It spans prompting methods, supervised fine-tuning on tool-call traces, and reinforcement learning against executable feedback.
The central insight is that many failures of standalone LLMs — arithmetic slips, stale facts, inability to act in the world — are not reasoning failures but
failures that the right tool resolves exactly. A calculator makes arithmetic exact; a search tool makes facts current; a code sandbox makes logic executable.

Tools convert an unreliable statistical guess into a deterministic computation. This matters because it moves the burden of correctness off the model's parameters and onto verifiable systems. The result is agents that are both more accurate and more auditable — every external effect is a logged, inspectable call.

It also matters for scale of capability. There are hundreds of thousands of public APIs. A model that can read a tool's schema and use it zero-shot generalises to functionality it never saw in training, which is far cheaper than fine-tuning a new skill into the weights for every task.
— the structured request/response protocol between model and tool.
— how tool inputs are described so the model can populate them.
— the two main ways tool skills are trained into weights.
— selecting the relevant few tools from a large library before calling.
Tool selection under a large library
When only a handful of tools exist, the model can see all their schemas at once. With thousands, the schemas exceed the context window, so a retrieval step first narrows the library to the top-
candidates by embedding similarity between the query and each tool's description.

Selecting the right tool is half the battle; the model must also fill arguments correctly from the query and prior observations. Argument errors — wrong types, missing required fields, hallucinated enum values — are a dominant failure mode and the main target of tool-use fine-tuning.

The distinctive training signal in tool learning is that a call either succeeds or throws. This binary, verifiable feedback is ideal for reinforcement learning: the reward is whether the tool executed and moved the task toward its goal, expressed as

Models such as Toolformer bootstrap this by self-labelling: they insert candidate API calls into text, keep only those calls that reduce the model's loss on the following tokens, and fine-tune on the survivors.

Real tasks chain tools: search, then read, then compute, then write. Composition raises the question of planning — sequencing calls so each output feeds the next — and error propagation, since a wrong early call corrupts everything downstream.

attrs.alt: Tool-learning pipeline: retrieve, select, ground, execute, integrate

content.text: The retrieval front-end is what makes the pipeline scale to large tool libraries; the execution feedback at the back-end is what makes it trainable. Between them sits the model's core job: mapping intent to a correctly grounded call.
A minimal tool-learning system defines tools by schema, retrieves relevant ones, and loops on execution. The example uses a retrieval step so the design survives growth from three tools to thousands.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Tool:
    name: str
    description: str
    schema: dict
    fn: callable

def build_tool(name, desc, schema, fn):
    return Tool(name, desc, schema, fn)

# A toy registry; in production this is a vector store keyed on descriptions.
REGISTRY = [
    build_tool("get_weather", "Current weather for a city",
               {"type": "object", "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["city"]},
               lambda city: f"{city}: 18C, clear"),
    build_tool("convert_currency", "Convert an amount between currencies",
               {"type": "object",
                "properties": {"amount": {"type": "number"},
                               "frm": {"type": "string"}, "to": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["amount", "frm", "to"]},
               lambda amount, frm, to: round(amount * 1.08, 2)),
]

def retrieve_tools(query, k=5):
    # Placeholder for embedding similarity search over tool descriptions.
    return REGISTRY[:k]
— benchmarks like ToolBench and API-Bank evaluate selection across thousands of real APIs.
— Toolformer-style methods that decide where a call helps using the model's own loss.
— agents that write and reuse their own tools (functions) when no existing tool fits.
— constrained decoding and schema-guided generation to eliminate malformed calls.
— planning and error recovery across many chained calls.

attrs.alt: The tool-use lifecycle from intent through grounded, repaired execution

content.text: Each stage has its own failure mode and its own metric: retrieval recall, selection accuracy, argument validity, and execution success. Diagnosing a tool-using agent means attributing failures to the correct stage rather than blaming the model wholesale — a low overall score usually traces to one weak link in this chain.
— floods the prompt and degrades selection; retrieve first.
— hiding a tool's exception denies the model the feedback it needs to retry correctly.
— the model selects on the description text, so imprecise wording causes wrong-tool errors.
— accepting malformed calls turns a recoverable schema error into a silent wrong answer.
Write tool descriptions as if they were the model's only documentation — precise, disambiguated, example-bearing.
Return structured, informative errors so the model can self-correct on the next step.
Retrieve a small candidate set before every call when the library is large.
Validate arguments against the schema before execution and feed validation failures back as observations.

Picture the model as a skilled operator sitting at a console of instruments. It does not need to compute a logarithm in its head or memorise today's exchange rate — it needs to know which instrument does that, how to set its dials, and how to read the display. Tool learning is training the operator's judgement about the console, not enlarging the operator's memory. A better operator is one who reaches for the right instrument and reads it correctly, not one who tries to do everything by hand.
correctness must be exact and verifiable — arithmetic, unit conversion, database lookups, code execution.
the answer depends on information outside the model's training data, such as current prices or private records.
an action has real-world effects that must be logged and auditable.
the task is pure natural-language generation the model already does well — an unnecessary call adds latency and failure modes.
the library is large; never inline thousands of schemas into the prompt.

Customer-support and operations agents are the canonical deployment. Such an agent might expose dozens of internal tools — look up an order, issue a refund, check inventory — retrieved from a registry of hundreds. The reliability challenge is argument grounding: a refund tool called with the wrong order id is worse than no tool at all, so production systems validate arguments against the schema and often require confirmation for irreversible actions.

The dominant cost at scale is not model inference but tool selection accuracy as the library grows. With a flat prompt, selection quality degrades once schemas exceed the context budget; with retrieval, selection accuracy depends on the quality of the embedding index and stays roughly constant in the number of tools. Latency is dominated by the slowest tool in a chain, so parallelising independent calls and caching idempotent results are the primary optimisations.
— the native structured-call interfaces of frontier model providers.
— a standard for exposing tools and data sources to agents uniformly.
— ToolBench, API-Bank, and BFCL measure selection and argument accuracy across many APIs.
— libraries that force outputs to conform to a JSON schema, eliminating malformed calls.
Every tool a model can call is a capability an attacker may try to trigger. Tool results are also untrusted text that can carry injected instructions. Harden both directions of the interface.
— expose only the tools a task needs, and scope credentials per call.
— treat tool outputs as data, delimiting them so a returned document cannot issue new instructions.
— require explicit approval for irreversible or high-impact actions.
— cap calls per tool to contain runaway loops and abuse.
tool use is only a prompting trick.
State-of-the-art tool use is trained into weights with SFT and RL against real execution feedback, not just prompted.
giving a model more tools always makes it more capable.
Beyond a small set, unretrieved tools crowd the context and hurt selection accuracy; scale needs retrieval.
a model that can reason can automatically use any API.
Argument grounding is a distinct, error-prone skill; schema-guided decoding and training are what make it reliable.
Tool learning teaches models whether, which, and how to call external tools, and how to chain their outputs.
Tools fix grounding failures — arithmetic, stale facts, real-world actions — by delegating to verifiable systems.
Execution feedback is a binary, verifiable reward, making tool use especially amenable to reinforcement learning.
Large tool libraries require a retrieval front-end; you cannot fit thousands of schemas in context.
Precise tool descriptions and informative errors are the highest-leverage levers for reliability.
